source: Zylos AI — Graceful Degradation Patterns in AI Agent Systems
summary: Documents a four-tier recovery cascade for production agent systems (retry → simplified prompt → fallback model → human queue), reporting 60-80% reduction in human escalation rates. Notes 41-86.7% baseline failure rates without deliberate fault tolerance.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — maps directly to the AIBlocked gap; ProcessRunner has no retry or fallback logic today

source: Mindra Blog — When Agents Fail: Engineering Fault-Tolerant AI Systems
summary: Practical engineering guide for layered resilience in multi-agent systems: circuit breakers, bulkheads, fallback chains, and self-healing state machines. Corroborates 4-level cascade pattern with exponential backoff.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — complements Zylos; both informed HID-132 design

source: MemRL — Self-Evolving Agents via Runtime RL on Episodic Memory (arxiv 2601.03192)
summary: Non-parametric framework that maintains an external memory store updated via Q-value scoring (temporal-difference RL) on task outcomes. Two-phase retrieval: semantic similarity then Q-value re-ranking. LLM weights never change. Strong benchmarks on BigCodeBench, ALFWorld, HLE.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly addresses no-agent-memory-across-runs limitation; Q-value retrieval is superior to cosine-only and fits PostgreSQL-only constraint

source: Braintrust — Best tools for tracking LLM costs in production (2026)
summary: Formalizes per-stage cost attribution as the foundational primitive for agent cost optimization. Introduces 'Goodput' metric and documents 60-90% cost reduction achievable via informed model routing once stage telemetry is in place.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — AgentForge has zero per-stage observability; this is the prerequisite for any future cost optimization

source: LangWatch — 4 best tools for monitoring LLM agent applications in 2026
summary: Multi-step trace visualization for agent workflows, per-stage latency/token tracking, and failure attribution. Part of the observability landscape that informed HID-133 requirements.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — secondary reference for telemetry issue; specific tooling is out of scope (AgentForge does this natively)

source: PASTE — Act While Thinking: Accelerating LLM Agents via Pattern-Aware Speculative Tool Execution (arxiv 2603.18897)
summary: Predicts and pre-executes likely future tool calls during LLM thinking time, hiding tool latency in the LLM-tool loop. 48.5% average task completion time reduction, 1.8x throughput. SJTU + Microsoft Research, March 2026.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — compelling latency innovation but AgentForge bottleneck is agent subprocess runtime, not tool latency within an agent; speculative prompt prefetch is a weaker form that wasn't selected this cycle

source: B-PASTE — Beam-Aware Pattern-Guided Speculative Execution for Resource-Constrained LLM Agents (arxiv 2604.16469)
summary: Extends PASTE with beam-aware speculation over local branch hypotheses under resource constraints. Very new (April 2026), limited production evidence yet.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — extends PASTE concept; too immature to evaluate for AgentForge implementation this cycle

source: Karpathy AutoResearch — AI agents running ML research in self-improving loop (github.com/karpathy/autoresearch)
summary: Infinite hill-climbing loop: AI agent modifies training code, runs 5-minute experiment, keeps improvements, reverts regressions, commits to git. 700+ experiments in 2 days, 20 validated optimizations. Released March 7 2026.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — inspirational for self-improvement concept but specific to ML training loops, not code orchestration; the git-commit-per-improvement pattern could inform AgentForge artifact versioning

source: SICA — A Self-Improving Coding Agent (self-editing prompts and tools from execution feedback)
summary: Agents that edit their own prompt templates and tool definitions based on execution feedback; Multi-Agent Reflexion variant has multiple agents reflect from different perspectives on shared failures.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — prompt self-editing could complement MemRL but adds complexity; not selected this cycle as MemRL covers the cross-run learning gap more directly

source: The Kitchen Loop — User-Spec-Driven Development for a Self-Evolving Codebase (arxiv 2603.25697)
summary: Self-improving coding system anchored to a user specification surface rather than an optimization objective; discovers its own infrastructure bugs and updates skill files. Spec-driven improvement prevents unconstrained self-modification.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — interesting spec-anchored approach but overlaps with AgentForge's existing Linear-issue-as-spec model; no net new capability identified this cycle

source: Budget-Constrained Agentic LLMs — BATS and Ares frameworks (arxiv 2602.11541, arxiv 2603.07915)
summary: BATS: budget-aware test-time scaling for tool-augmented agents; Ares: adaptive reasoning effort selection based on task complexity. Both reduce token consumption on simple tasks while preserving quality on hard ones.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — valuable once telemetry (HID-133) establishes per-stage cost baselines; premature without that data

source: Ares: Adaptive Reasoning Effort Selection for Efficient LLM Agents (arxiv 2603.07915)
summary: Dynamically selects reasoning effort (model size, chain-of-thought depth) based on estimated task complexity; reduces cost without quality degradation for straightforward tasks. March 2026.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — synergistic with HID-133 telemetry; consider re-evaluation once per-stage cost data exists

source: Hermes Agent — Nous Research self-improving CLI agent with persistent memory
summary: Self-improving CLI agent with persistent memory, automated skill creation, sandboxed code execution via Unix socket RPC, multi-platform reach. Supports 300+ models.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — architectural mismatch; Hermes is a standalone agent framework whereas AgentForge orchestrates external CLI agents (Claude Code, Codex). Memory and skill concepts partially overlapping with HID-134.

source: Episodic-Semantic Memory Architecture for Long-Horizon Scientific Agents (arxiv 2605.17625)
summary: Combines episodic (event-specific) and semantic (generalized fact) memory in a dual-store architecture for scientific agents operating over long horizons. May 2026, very recent.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — dual-store concept is richer than MemRL's single store but adds implementation complexity; MemRL was selected as the simpler starting point that preserves PostgreSQL-only constraint
